neighborhoods.name: East Dayton / West Third Street Corridor
primary_groups: Appalachian migrants from eastern Kentucky, West Virginia, and southern Ohio
neighborhoods.character: The primary landing zone for Appalachian migrants who came to Dayton for factory work during and after World War II. The West Third Street corridor running east from downtown became the spine of Appalachian settlement — cheap rooming houses, storefront churches, and country music bars lined the strip. Migrants clustered by county of origin, and the neighborhood had the feel of a displaced mountain community transplanted to flat Ohio ground.
key_institutions: Country music bars along West Third Street
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Dayton from {origin_state}, East Dayton along West Third Street was where the familiar accents were — a strip of rooming houses, storefront churches, and honky-tonks that felt like a mountain county relocated to the Miami Valley.

neighborhoods.name: West Dayton
primary_groups: African Americans from the Deep South and Upper South
neighborhoods.character: Dayton's primary Black neighborhood, centered on West Fifth Street and the surrounding blocks. Great Migration arrivals from Alabama, Georgia, and Tennessee found community here, though restrictive covenants and redlining kept the neighborhood's boundaries rigid. The commercial district along West Fifth Street served as the cultural and economic heart of Black Dayton, with churches, barbershops, and small businesses packed into a compressed geography.
key_institutions: Dunbar Branch Library (named for Dayton native Paul Laurence Dunbar)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Black and came to Dayton, West Fifth Street was the center of gravity — the neighborhood where Paul Laurence Dunbar had grown up a generation earlier and where every arriving family from the South found a church, a barber, and somebody who knew somebody from back home.

neighborhoods.name: South Park
primary_groups: Working-class whites, Hungarian and German immigrants, later Appalachian migrants
neighborhoods.character: A working-class residential neighborhood south of downtown, originally settled by Hungarian and German immigrant factory workers. Modest frame houses on small lots. By the 1940s, Appalachian migrants began moving into South Park's affordable housing stock. The neighborhood was defined by its proximity to industrial employers along the Great Miami River.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: South Park was the kind of neighborhood where {ancestor_name} could afford to buy a house on a factory wage — modest frame homes on small lots, within walking distance of the plants along the Great Miami River.

neighborhoods.name: Dayton View
primary_groups: Middle-class professionals, NCR employees, skilled tradesmen
neighborhoods.character: A respectable middle-class neighborhood north of downtown that represented the next step up for families who had done well in the factories or moved into white-collar work at NCR. Tree-lined streets with larger homes and a more settled feel than the working-class neighborhoods closer to the industrial core. NCR executives and engineers were a visible presence.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family moved to Dayton View, they had made it — the tree-lined streets north of downtown were where factory families went after a generation of saving, or where an NCR clerk could afford a house that said the family was doing well.

major_employers.name: National Cash Register Company (NCR)
major_employers.wages: Factory workers: $2-4/day (1900s-1910s), rising to $0.75-1.25/hr (1940s). NCR was known for above-average wages and paternalistic employee benefits under founder John H. Patterson.
major_employers.working_conditions: NCR was Dayton's defining employer and one of America's most innovative companies. John H. Patterson pioneered welfare capitalism — the company provided workers with parks, gardens, dining halls, and educational programs. The NCR factory campus south of downtown was a landmark. Patterson's management innovations influenced American business practice nationwide, though his labor relations could be autocratic.
major_employers.narrative_hook: NCR was the company that made Dayton — if {ancestor_name} worked there in the {decade}s, they were part of an operation that John H. Patterson ran like a small city, with company gardens, dining halls, and the expectation that workers would improve themselves on the boss's schedule.

major_employers.name: Delco / General Motors (Frigidaire, Inland Division, Delco Products)
major_employers.wages: UAW contracts from the late 1930s brought rising wages: $1.00-1.50/hr (1940s), $2.50-4.00/hr (1960s). Benefits included health insurance, pensions, and paid vacations.
major_employers.working_conditions: Charles Kettering's Delco (Dayton Engineering Laboratories Company) put Dayton on the automotive map. After GM acquired Delco, multiple GM divisions operated plants across Dayton — Frigidaire made appliances, Inland Division made steering components, Delco Products made shock absorbers. At peak employment in the 1960s, GM was Dayton's largest private employer. The UAW local was a powerful force in the community. Plant closures beginning in the 1970s devastated the local economy.
major_employers.narrative_hook: GM was everywhere in Dayton — Frigidaire, Inland, Delco Products — and if {ancestor_name} hired on at any of them in the {decade}s, the UAW card meant a middle-class life that would have been unimaginable back in {origin_state}.

major_employers.name: Wright-Patterson Air Force Base
major_employers.wages: Federal civil service pay scales. Stable employment with benefits and pensions that compared favorably to private-sector factory work.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Wright Brothers' hometown became a center of military aviation research and logistics. Wright-Patterson (originally Wright Field and Patterson Field, merged 1948) was one of the largest Air Force installations in the country. The base provided stable civilian employment that was less vulnerable to the boom-and-bust cycles of manufacturing. For Appalachian migrants, a civil service job at the base represented exceptional security.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Wright-Patterson was the steady paycheck in a factory town — if {ancestor_name} landed a civilian job at the base, the federal benefits and pension were the most secure employment Dayton had to offer, and the Wright Brothers' legacy hung over every hangar.

major_employers.name: Mead Corporation (Paper)
major_employers.wages: Mill workers earned competitive industrial wages. Mead was considered a solid employer with steady work.
major_employers.working_conditions: Mead Corporation, headquartered in Dayton, was one of the nation's major paper producers. The company's local operations included paper mills and the corporate headquarters. Paper mill work was hot, wet, and noisy, but the company was a reliable employer in a city dominated by metalworking and automotive parts.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} worked at Mead, the paper mill offered something different from the auto plants — steady work in an industry that did not live and die by the next GM contract.

major_employers.name: McCall's Printing / McCall Corporation
major_employers.wages: Printing trades were skilled work — pressmen and typesetters earned above-average industrial wages, particularly under union contracts.
major_employers.working_conditions: McCall's operated one of the largest printing plants in the country in Dayton, producing the McCall's pattern and magazine empire. The printing trades were among the most skilled industrial jobs available, and the plant employed a large workforce. Dayton's printing industry gave the city an economic base beyond metalworking.
major_employers.narrative_hook: McCall's printing plant was one of Dayton's signature employers — if {ancestor_name} had a skilled trade in the printing industry, the Dayton operation was among the largest in the nation.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Appalachian migrants typically arrived with little capital and rented rooms in boarding houses or doubled up with kin who had come earlier. The West Third Street corridor in East Dayton was the primary entry point — cheap rooming houses and small apartments that could be rented by the week. Some migrants maintained ties to home counties and traveled back and forth seasonally before settling permanently.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Appalachian migrants clustered in East Dayton and along the West Third Street corridor, often grouping by county of origin. Black residents were confined to West Dayton by restrictive covenants and discriminatory lending practices. Older European immigrant communities — Hungarian, German — were concentrated in South Park and the near south side. The pattern was less rigid than in larger cities, but the color line was firmly drawn.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Rooming house or shared rental (weeks to months) to independent rental (1-3 years) to modest homeownership (3-10 years). Dayton's housing stock was more affordable than Cleveland, Cincinnati, or Detroit, making homeownership achievable for many working families within a single generation. Appalachian migrants who held steady factory jobs often purchased modest frame houses in East Dayton or South Park within five years of arrival.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Appalachian migrants in Dayton faced the 'hillbilly' stigma common across the industrial Midwest — stereotyped as ignorant, clannish, and prone to violence. They were sometimes called 'briar hoppers' by established residents. Black migrants faced the more systematic discrimination of restrictive covenants, employment ceilings, and segregated public accommodations that persisted into the 1960s.
specific_incidents: 1966 West Dayton riots — civil disturbance driven by frustration over housing discrimination and police practices
Persistent 'hillbilly' stigma against Appalachian migrants through the 1950s-1960s
Redlining maps designated West Dayton as high-risk, limiting Black homeownership opportunities
nativist_organizations: KKK presence in Montgomery County during the 1920s
West Dayton restrictive covenant enforcement by neighborhood associations

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Wayman AME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community in West Dayton
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism and marriage records. One of the oldest Black congregations in Dayton.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Black and settled in Dayton, Wayman AME was one of the anchor churches — a congregation old enough to have served free Blacks before the Civil War and strong enough to welcome every new wave from the South.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Storefront Churches (Appalachian Pentecostal and Baptist)
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Records are fragmentary and held by individual congregations if they survive. Many storefront churches were informal and short-lived, but some grew into established congregations.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The storefront churches along West Third Street were where {ancestor_name} would have felt most at home — a preacher from back in the hills, singing the old hymns, in a converted shop front that smelled like home cooking and floor wax.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Dayton Urban League
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Case files and organizational records at Wright State University Special Collections. The Urban League helped Black migrants find employment and housing.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Dayton Urban League was the practical arm of the Great Migration — if {ancestor_name} needed a job lead or help navigating a segregated housing market, the League was often the first door to knock on.

source_references.author: Carl M. Engel
source_references.title: Dayton, Ohio: An Intimate History
source_references.notes: Local history covering Dayton's growth from a small Miami Valley town to a major industrial center, with attention to the communities and institutions that shaped the city.

source_references.author: Todd Michney
source_references.title: Surrogate Suburbs: Black Upward Mobility and Neighborhood Change in Cleveland, 1900-1980
source_references.notes: Though focused on Cleveland, provides essential context for understanding Black residential patterns and housing discrimination across Ohio's industrial cities, applicable to Dayton's West Dayton experience.

source_references.author: Phillip J. Obermiller, Thomas E. Wagner, and E. Bruce Tucker
source_references.title: Appalachian Odyssey: Historical Perspectives on the Great Migration
source_references.notes: Essential collection of essays on Appalachian out-migration to Midwestern cities, with material directly relevant to the Dayton experience of eastern Kentucky and West Virginia migrants.

source_references.author: Mark Bernstein
source_references.title: Grand Eccentrics: Turning the Century: Dayton and the Inventing of America
source_references.notes: Covers Dayton's remarkable turn-of-the-century innovation cluster — the Wright Brothers, John H. Patterson at NCR, Charles Kettering at Delco — and the industrial culture that drew workers to the city.
